content:
First, we show an upper‐bound of 250 000, by a simple “no two in a 2×2 block’’ argument.  Partition the 1000×1000 board into disjoint 2×2 squares.  In any single 2×2 square one checks easily that you cannot place two non‐adjacent dominoes—hence at most one domino per 2×2 block.  Since there are (1000/2)²=500²=250 000 such blocks, no more than 250 000 dominoes can be placed.

Next, we exhibit a placement achieving 250 000.  Tile the board by 250×250 copies of a 4×4 “super‐block’’ and in each 4×4 block place exactly four dominoes as follows (coordinates inside the block run from 1 to 4 in each direction):

1) A horizontal domino covering (1,2)–(1,3).  
2) A horizontal domino covering (4,2)–(4,3).  
3) A vertical domino covering (2,1)–(3,1).  
4) A vertical domino covering (2,4)–(3,4).

One checks that within each 4×4 block no two of these four dominoes touch edge‐to‐edge, and that dominoes in adjacent 4×4 blocks remain at least one empty layer apart.  Thus this pattern is globally legal, and uses exactly 4 dominoes per 16 cells, i.e. ¼ of the cells are covered by dominoes, so in total

   (1000·1000)/16 · 4  = 250 000

dominoes.

Hence the maximum possible is

\boxed{250000}.

message.content:
First, we show an upper‐bound of 250 000, by a simple “no two in a 2×2 block’’ argument.  Partition the 1000×1000 board into disjoint 2×2 squares.  In any single 2×2 square one checks easily that you cannot place two non‐adjacent dominoes—hence at most one domino per 2×2 block.  Since there are (1000/2)²=500²=250 000 such blocks, no more than 250 000 dominoes can be placed.

Next, we exhibit a placement achieving 250 000.  Tile the board by 250×250 copies of a 4×4 “super‐block’’ and in each 4×4 block place exactly four dominoes as follows (coordinates inside the block run from 1 to 4 in each direction):

1) A horizontal domino covering (1,2)–(1,3).  
2) A horizontal domino covering (4,2)–(4,3).  
3) A vertical domino covering (2,1)–(3,1).  
4) A vertical domino covering (2,4)–(3,4).

One checks that within each 4×4 block no two of these four dominoes touch edge‐to‐edge, and that dominoes in adjacent 4×4 blocks remain at least one empty layer apart.  Thus this pattern is globally legal, and uses exactly 4 dominoes per 16 cells, i.e. ¼ of the cells are covered by dominoes, so in total

   (1000·1000)/16 · 4  = 250 000

dominoes.

Hence the maximum possible is

\boxed{250000}.
